title: Florida Workers' Compensation Law: Who Is Required to Have It?
summary: Florida has some of the strictest workers' comp requirements in the country — especially for construction businesses. Here's exactly who must carry coverage and what happens if you don't.
body:
Workers' compensation insurance isn't optional for most Florida businesses — it's the law. But the rules vary significantly by industry, and many business owners are surprised to learn just how broad the requirements are.

General Rule: 4 or More Employees

For most Florida businesses, workers' compensation coverage is required once you have 4 or more employees — whether they're full-time, part-time, or seasonal. It doesn't matter how many hours they work; if they're on your payroll, they count.

Construction: The Strictest Standard

Florida construction businesses face the toughest requirements in the state. Workers' comp is required for any business in the construction industry with even one employee — including the business owner in many cases. This applies to:

  • General contractors and subcontractors
  • Roofing companies
  • Plumbers, electricians, HVAC technicians
  • Landscapers who use power equipment
  • Any trade classified under construction SIC codes

What About Sole Proprietors and Partners?

Sole proprietors and partners in non-construction businesses are generally excluded from mandatory coverage — but they can elect to cover themselves voluntarily. In construction, the rules are much stricter: owners may be included or required to carry coverage depending on the structure of the business.

Penalties for Non-Compliance

Florida's Department of Financial Services actively enforces workers' comp requirements. Penalties for non-compliance include:

  • Stop-work orders requiring you to halt all business operations immediately
  • Fines of up to $1,000 per day from the date coverage was required
  • Personal liability for any injuries that occur while uninsured

Inspectors routinely visit job sites, and if you can't show proof of coverage, the stop-work order comes first and questions come later.

Get Covered Today

title: How Florida Workers' Comp Class Codes Affect Your Premium
summary: Your workers' comp class code is one of the most important factors in your premium. Learn how class codes work, how they're assigned, and how to make sure your business isn't overpaying.
body:
If you've ever wondered why one Florida contractor pays dramatically different workers' comp rates than another doing seemingly similar work, the answer is almost always class codes. Understanding how they work can save your business real money.

What Is a Workers' Comp Class Code?

A class code (also called a classification code) is a 4-digit number assigned to every type of work by the National Council on Compensation Insurance (NCCI). Each code represents a category of job type with an associated risk level — and that risk level directly determines your base rate.

For example:

  • Code 5183 — Plumbing: carries a moderate rate because plumbers face falls and tool injuries
  • Code 5551 — Roofing: one of the highest-rate codes in Florida due to extreme fall risk
  • Code 8810 — Clerical office employees: very low rate since office workers rarely suffer injuries

How Rates Are Calculated

Your workers' comp premium is calculated using this basic formula:

Premium = (Payroll ÷ 100) × Class Code Rate × Experience Modifier

The class code rate is set per $100 of payroll. If your roofing company has $500,000 in annual payroll and your class code rate is $15 per $100, your base premium would be $75,000 before any modifiers apply.

Common Class Code Mistakes That Cost You Money

Many businesses are placed in the wrong class code — often a more expensive one. Common errors include:

  • Lumping all employees under a single construction code when some do only clerical work
  • Using a residential construction code for commercial work (or vice versa)
  • Not separating out drivers, superintendents, or sales staff who rarely do field work

An experienced workers' comp agent will audit your class codes at renewal and make sure you're not overpaying.

Let Us Review Your Policy

The FLAWC.com team reviews class code assignments as part of every quote. Call us at 800-616-1418 or get a free quote — we'll compare your current classification against what your business actually does.

title: What to Do When an Employee Is Injured on the Job in Florida
summary: A workplace injury doesn't have to become a disaster. This step-by-step guide walks Florida employers through exactly what to do in the first 24 hours and beyond.
body:
Even with the best safety practices, workplace accidents happen. How you respond in the first hours after an injury determines whether the claim goes smoothly — or turns into a prolonged legal and financial headache. Here's what every Florida employer needs to know.

Step 1: Ensure Safety and Get Medical Attention

Your first priority is the injured employee's safety. If the injury is serious, call 911 immediately. For non-emergency injuries, direct the employee to a medical provider authorized by your workers' comp carrier — not just any emergency room. Using an unauthorized provider can complicate the claim significantly.

Keep a list of authorized treating physicians posted in your workplace.

Step 2: Report the Injury to Your Insurance Carrier

Florida law requires employers to report workplace injuries to their workers' comp carrier within 7 days of becoming aware of the injury. Missing this deadline can result in penalties and complications with the claim.

When you report, be prepared to provide:

  • Employee name, job title, and contact information
  • Date, time, and location of the incident
  • A description of what happened and the nature of the injury
  • Names of any witnesses

Step 3: Document Everything

Create a written incident report immediately — while details are fresh. Take photos of the scene if relevant. Gather witness statements. This documentation protects both the employee and the business if the claim is disputed later.

Step 4: Cooperate with the Claims Process

Once a claim is filed, your carrier will assign a claims adjuster. Cooperate fully and respond promptly to their requests. The faster information flows, the faster the claim resolves — and the less your experience modifier (EMR) is affected.

Step 5: Focus on Return-to-Work

Florida carriers and NCCI strongly favor employers who have active return-to-work programs. Offering a modified-duty position to an injured employee can significantly reduce claim costs and your long-term premium impact.

Make Sure Your Policy Is Right

The time to learn about your workers' comp claims process is before an injury, not after. The FLAWC.com team helps Florida employers understand their coverage — call us at 800-616-1418 for a free policy review.

title: Experience Modification Rate (EMR): How It Works and How to Lower It
summary: Your EMR is the single biggest factor in your workers' comp premium after class codes. A high EMR can add tens of thousands of dollars to your annual cost. Here's how to bring it down.
body:
Of all the factors that affect your workers' compensation premium, the Experience Modification Rate — commonly called the EMR or experience mod — may be the most impactful and least understood. For Florida businesses with significant payroll, a high EMR can add tens of thousands of dollars to annual insurance costs.

What Is the EMR?

The EMR is a multiplier applied to your workers' comp base premium that reflects your actual claims history compared to businesses in the same industry. An EMR of 1.0 is average — meaning you pay the standard rate. An EMR below 1.0 means you're safer than average and get a discount. Above 1.0 means you've had more claims than average and pay a surcharge.

For example, if your base workers' comp premium is $50,000:

  • EMR of 0.85 → You pay $42,500 (15% discount)
  • EMR of 1.0 → You pay $50,000 (no change)
  • EMR of 1.25 → You pay $62,500 (25% surcharge)

How Is the EMR Calculated?

NCCI calculates your EMR annually using your loss history from the most recent 3 years (excluding the most current year). Both the frequency and severity of claims matter, but frequency typically has a larger impact — meaning many small claims can hurt your EMR more than one large claim.

How to Lower Your EMR

Improving your EMR is a multi-year process, but the results are real:

  • Invest in workplace safety: OSHA training, regular equipment inspections, and safety protocols reduce the frequency of claims.
  • Implement a return-to-work program: Getting injured employees back to light duty faster reduces claim severity and duration.
  • Review claims actively: Work with your carrier to close open claims promptly. Reserve amounts affect your EMR even for claims still in progress.
  • Audit your EMR calculation: NCCI EMR calculations sometimes contain errors. An independent review can catch mistakes that inflate your mod.
  • Separate high-risk and low-risk employees: Properly classifying employees (as discussed in our class code article) ensures only the right workers are included in high-rate calculations.

Take Control of Your Workers' Comp Costs

The FLAWC.com team works with Florida businesses to understand and improve their EMR over time. If your EMR is above 1.0, let's talk about a plan to bring it down. Call 800-616-1418 or get a free quote today.

title: Ghost Policies and Florida Workers' Comp: What Business Owners Need to Know
summary: Ghost policies are a common but often misunderstood tool in Florida workers' comp — especially for sole proprietors. Here's what they are, when they're legitimate, and the risks to watch for.
body:
If you've worked in Florida construction or hired subcontractors, you've probably heard the term "ghost policy." It's one of the most commonly discussed — and most frequently misused — concepts in Florida workers' compensation. Here's what you need to know.

What Is a Ghost Policy?

A ghost policy (sometimes called a "named exclusion policy") is a workers' compensation policy issued to a sole proprietor or small LLC where the owner formally excludes themselves from coverage. The result is a policy that technically satisfies the state's requirement to have workers' comp, but covers zero employees because the only person on the policy has opted out.

The policy is real — it just doesn't cover anyone in practice, which is why it's called a "ghost" policy.

When Are Ghost Policies Legitimate?

There are legitimate uses for ghost policies in Florida. A sole proprietor who has no employees but is required to provide a certificate of insurance to a general contractor or project owner may obtain a ghost policy to meet contractual requirements. In this case, the sole proprietor is accepting personal liability for any injuries they suffer on the job — a calculated risk some business owners are willing to take.

The Risks and Red Flags

Ghost policies become problematic when they're used to misrepresent a business's actual workforce. Common abuses include:

  • Misclassifying employees as 1099 contractors: Just because someone is paid as an independent contractor doesn't mean they legally are one under Florida law. If they're found to be employees during a claim or audit, you bear full liability.
  • Hiring subs with ghost policies to avoid payroll: General contractors who hire subcontractors with ghost policies may find themselves liable for injuries those subs suffer on the job.
  • Fraudulent certificates: Some contractors present ghost policy certificates deceptively, implying real coverage exists. This can expose all parties to fraud liability.

What General Contractors Should Do

If you're a GC hiring subcontractors, don't just collect certificates of insurance — verify them. Call the carrier and confirm the policy is active and actually covers the workers doing your job. The Florida Division of Workers' Compensation has an online lookup tool for this purpose.

Questions? We Can Help

Workers' comp compliance in Florida can be complicated, especially when subcontractors and 1099s are involved. The FLAWC.com team helps Florida businesses navigate these questions every day. Call 800-616-1418 for a free consultation.
